meta.description: CGI/IUGS controlled vocabulary of fault types used in GeoSciML ShearDisplacementStructure descriptions. Concepts are arranged in a SKOS hierarchy with 'fault' as the top concept.

concepts.definition: A discrete surface or zone of discrete surfaces, with some thickness, separating two rock masses across which one mass has slid past the other, and characterized by brittle deformation.
A fault with a dip-slip movement where the hanging wall has moved downward relative to the footwall. Normal faults are associated with extensional tectonic settings.
A fault with reverse-slip movement where the hanging wall moves upward relative to the footwall. Dip of fault plane is greater than 45 degrees. Associated with compressional tectonic regimes.
A reverse fault with a shallow dip (typically less than 45 degrees) along which older rocks are thrust over younger rocks. Characteristic of fold-and-thrust belts.
A fault where displacement is predominantly parallel to the strike of the fault surface (horizontal movement). Divided into dextral and sinistral varieties based on sense of movement.
A strike-slip fault where the block on the opposite side of the fault moves to the right when viewed across the fault plane.
A strike-slip fault where the block on the opposite side of the fault moves to the left when viewed across the fault plane.
A fault that has significant components of both dip-slip and strike-slip displacement. Net slip vector is oblique to both strike and dip of the fault surface.

A normal fault that is curved in cross-section, becoming less steep with depth. The fault plane is concave upward, typically flattening into a detachment horizon. Common in rifted continental margins.
A large-displacement, low-angle normal fault that forms the base of an extended terrain. The detachment surface separates an upper plate from a lower plate and may expose metamorphic core complexes.
A strike-slip fault that is a plate boundary, connecting two other plate boundaries (typically mid-ocean ridges or subduction zones). Movement is parallel to the plate boundary.
A large-scale, steeply dipping strike-slip fault that cuts through the crust and may be hundreds of kilometers long. A continental-scale strike-slip fault that crosses structural trends.
A fault that does not reach the Earth's surface. The fault terminates below the surface and its displacement is accommodated by folding of the overlying strata, forming a detachment fold.
A syn-sedimentary fault that was active during deposition, resulting in thicker strata on the downthrown (hanging wall) side. Common in deltaic and passive margin settings; forms rollover anticlines.
A fault that transfers displacement between two sub-parallel extensional or contractional fault systems. In rift settings, transfer faults are often strike-slip and connect normal fault segments.
